title: AcyclicHardInterventionTopologicalOrder
tex_block_html: If a CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic then also $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is acyclic and a topological order for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is also one for $G$. Any topological order of $G$ can be extended to one for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, e.g.\ by putting all the $I_w$ nodes first in the ordering.

\phantomsection\label{claim_3_13}

\begin{Rem}[AcyclicHardInterventionTopologicalOrder]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg} and recall the typing and axioms of def \ref{def-cdmg}, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \ins V \times V$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$), and $L$ symmetric ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$). Let
    \[ W \ins J \cup V \]
    be a subset of nodes, and let
    \[ G_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; \lp J_{\doit(I_W)},\, V_{\doit(I_W)},\, E_{\doit(I_W)},\, L_{\doit(I_W)} \rp \]
    be the extended CDMG of $G$ w.r.t.\ nodes $W$ and corresponding intervention nodes $I_W$, as constructed in def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}. Both quantifiers ``for every CDMG $G$'' and ``for every $W \ins J \cup V$'' are read \emph{universally}, with the side condition $W \ins J \cup V$ inherited verbatim from def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes} (so $W$ is permitted to overlap the input-node set $J$, i.e.\ $W \cap J \neq \emptyset$ is admitted); the case $W = \emptyset$ is admitted as well (then $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC = \emptyset$ and $G_{\doit(I_W)} = G$ verbatim by items i.--iv.\ of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}).

    \emph{Notational shorthand recap from def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}.}
    Per def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, the family of intervention symbols is $I_W = \lC I_w \st w \in W \rC$ with the convention $I_j := j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ (a purely notational shorthand: no new node is introduced on the $J \cap W$ branch, the pre-existing input node $j \in J$ plays the role of its own intervention node, and the pre-existing edges incident to $j$ in $G$ are retained unchanged in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$). Consequently $I_W$ decomposes as
    \[
        I_W \;=\; (J \cap W) \;\cup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC,
    \]
    where the first component contains the (notational) $I_j = j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ and the second component contains the genuinely fresh symbols $I_w$ for $w \in W \sm J$, the latter type-disjoint from $J \cup V$ and from each other by the freshness clause of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}. We henceforth refer to the elements of $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ as the \emph{fresh intervention nodes} of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.

    \emph{Carrier of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.}
    By items i.\ and ii.\ of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes},
    \[
        J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; \lp J \cup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \rp \cup V \;=\; (J \cup V) \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC,
    \]
    where the second equality is a type-level disjoint union by the freshness clause of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes} ($\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$). In particular, $J \cup V$ is a subset of $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ via the canonical set-theoretic inclusion
    \[
        \iota \,:\, J \cup V \;\hookrightarrow\; J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}, \qquad v \;\longmapsto\; v,
    \]
    and the inclusion is strict when $W \sm J \neq \emptyset$ (the fresh intervention nodes belong to the codomain but not to the domain) and is an equality when $W \sm J = \emptyset$. In the downstream Lean rendering of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes} this inclusion is realised as the ``unsplit'' constructor of a tagged-sum carrier; the underlying set-theoretic content is plain inclusion.

    \emph{Topological orders live on different carriers.}
    By def \ref{def-topological-order}, a topological order of $G$ is a strict total order $<_G$ on $J \cup V$, while a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is a strict total order $<_{G_{\doit(I_W)}}$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. When $W \sm J \neq \emptyset$, these carriers are not equal -- $<_{G_{\doit(I_W)}}$ is a relation on the strictly larger set $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ -- so a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is not literally a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}; the bridging operations are \emph{restriction} (sub-claim~(b) below) and \emph{extension} (sub-claim~(c) below), both taken along $\iota$.

    \emph{Reading of ``extend''.}
    Following standard mathematical usage, we read ``extension of a strict total order'' in the \emph{strict} sense: a strict total order $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ \emph{extends} a strict total order $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ when its restriction to $J \cup V$ along $\iota$ recovers $<_G$, i.e.\ for every $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$,
    \[
        v_1 \,<_G\, v_2 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \iota(v_1) \,\prec\, \iota(v_2).
    \]
    This is the strict reading; the weaker reading ``some strict total order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ whose ground set contains $J \cup V$'' is too lax to constrain the construction and is not adopted here.

    \emph{The remark.} The following conjunction of three sub-claims holds.

    \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
        \item \emph{Acyclicity is preserved by adding intervention nodes.}
              If $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}, then $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}, i.e.\ for every $x \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} = (J \cup V) \dcup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$, there does not exist any non-trivial directed walk from $x$ to itself in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$. The hypothesis ``$G$ is acyclic'' is used in this sub-claim; sub-claims~(b) and~(c) below do not require acyclicity of $G$.

        \item \emph{Restriction direction: every topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ restricts to one of $G$ along $\iota$.}
              For every strict total order $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$, if $\prec$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order} (i.e.\ for every $v, w \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$, $v \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(w) \Rightarrow v \prec w$), then the binary relation $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ defined by
              \[
                  v_1 \,<_G\, v_2 \;\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \iota(v_1) \,\prec\, \iota(v_2) \qquad \text{for } v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V
              \]
              is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}. The LN's prose ``a topological order for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is also one for $G$'' is read \emph{universally} (every such $\prec$, not merely some such $\prec$) and refers to this restriction along $\iota$: the carriers differ when $W \sm J \neq \emptyset$, so an order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ cannot literally be an order on $J \cup V$, but its restriction along $\iota$ is.

        \item \emph{Extension direction: every topological order of $G$ extends to one of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.}
              For every strict total order $<_G$ on $J \cup V$, if $<_G$ is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}, then there exists a strict total order $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ such that
              \begin{itemize}
                  \item $\prec$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}; and
                  \item $\prec$ \emph{extends} $<_G$ along $\iota$ in the strict sense recorded above, i.e.\ for every $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$, $v_1 <_G v_2 \Leftrightarrow \iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_2)$.
              \end{itemize}
              The LN's prose ``any topological order of $G$ can be extended to one for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$'' is read \emph{universally} (every such $<_G$ extends) and ``extended'' is read in the strict sense.

              \emph{Example construction (the LN's ``put all $I_w$ first'').}
              A concrete witness $\prec$ for the existential of sub-claim~(c) is the following ``fresh intervention nodes first'' relation: for $x, y \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} = (J \cup V) \dcup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$,
              \[
                  x \,\prec\, y \;\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
                  \begin{cases}
                      w_1 \,<_G\, w_2 & \text{if } x = I_{w_1} \text{ and } y = I_{w_2} \text{ for some } w_1, w_2 \in W \sm J, \\
                      \text{true} & \text{if } x = I_{w} \text{ for some } w \in W \sm J \text{ and } y \in J \cup V, \\
                      \text{false} & \text{if } x \in J \cup V \text{ and } y = I_{w} \text{ for some } w \in W \sm J, \\
                      v_1 \,<_G\, v_2 & \text{if } x = v_1 \text{ and } y = v_2 \text{ for some } v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V.
                  \end{cases}
              \]
              The four cases exhaust the disjoint-union carrier $(J \cup V) \dcup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ and are pairwise mutually exclusive, so the right-hand side is well-defined. In the first case the right-hand side $w_1 <_G w_2$ uses that $W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$ -- both target nodes $w_1, w_2$ lie in $V \ins J \cup V$, the carrier of $<_G$. In words: all fresh intervention nodes $I_w$ (i.e.\ those with $w \in W \sm J$) precede all elements of $J \cup V$ under $\prec$; among the fresh intervention nodes, $\prec$ is the $<_G$-order of their target nodes; among the elements of $J \cup V$, $\prec$ is $<_G$ itself. The restriction of $\prec$ to $J \cup V$ along $\iota$ is the fourth case, which is $<_G$ verbatim, so the strict extension property is satisfied by construction.
    \end{enumerate}

    \emph{What ``putting all the $I_w$ nodes first'' refers to (resolving the literal reading of the LN's prose).}
    By the notational shorthand $I_j := j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ recalled above from def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, the literal LN phrase ``putting all the $I_w$ nodes first'' must be read as ``putting all the \emph{fresh} intervention nodes first'', i.e.\ only the $I_w$ with $w \in W \sm J$ are placed at the front of the ordering; the $I_j = j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ are not separate nodes, they coincide with pre-existing input nodes of $G$, and they retain their $<_G$-position inside the $J \cup V$-block of $\prec$. This is the only reading consistent with the strict ``extends'' property of the previous paragraph: were the $J \cap W$ nodes also moved to the front, then the restriction of $\prec$ to $J \cup V$ along $\iota$ would in general differ from $<_G$ (it would reshuffle the $J \cap W$ nodes), violating the strict extension condition.

    \emph{Order among the fresh intervention nodes is a design freedom.}
    The LN's prose does not specify the relative order \emph{among} the fresh intervention nodes $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$. Any choice that makes $\prec$ a strict total order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ and respects the parent-precedence requirement of def \ref{def-topological-order} for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is admissible. The example construction above selects the specific choice ``$I_{w_1} \prec I_{w_2} \Leftrightarrow w_1 <_G w_2$'' (using that $W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$, so $<_G$ is defined on the targets); but any strict total order on $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ would equally well discharge the topological-order requirement for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, because the fresh intervention nodes are pairwise non-adjacent in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$: each fresh $I_w$ has the unique outgoing edge $(I_w, w) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ pointing to its target $w \in V$, has no incoming edges in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (by the freshness clause of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, no element of $E_{\doit(I_W)}$ has a fresh $I_w$ as its target), and the set $\lC (I_w, w) \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ contains no pair $(I_{w_1}, I_{w_2})$ with both endpoints fresh. Sub-claim~(c) asserts \emph{existence} of an extension; the example construction above provides one explicit witness.

    \emph{Role of $L$.}
    The bidirected-edge set $L$ plays no role in any of the three sub-claims: acyclicity (def \ref{def-acylic}) is defined via the non-existence of non-trivial directed walks (which use only $E$), and a topological order (def \ref{def-topological-order}) is defined via the parent-set $\Pa^G$ (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i., which is defined from $E$ alone). By item iv.\ of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, $L_{\doit(I_W)} = L$ is transported verbatim, but neither sub-claim consults it.
\end{Rem}

\end{document}

The example construction above selects the specific choice “$I_{w_1} \prec I_{w_2} \Leftrightarrow w_1 <_G w_2$” (using that $W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$, so $<_G$ is defined on the targets); but any strict total order on $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ would equally well discharge the topological-order requirement for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, because the fresh intervention nodes are pairwise non-adjacent in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$: each fresh $I_w$ has the unique outgoing edge $(I_w, w) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ pointing to its target $w \in V$, has no incoming edges in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (by the freshness clause of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, no element of $E_{\doit(I_W)}$ has a fresh $I_w$ as its target), and the set $\lC (I_w, w) \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ contains no pair $(I_{w_1}, I_{w_2})$ with both endpoints fresh. Sub-claim (c) asserts existence of an extension; the example construction above provides one explicit witness.

Role of $L$. The bidirected-edge set $L$ plays no role in any of the three sub-claims: acyclicity (def def-acylic) is defined via the non-existence of non-trivial directed walks (which use only $E$), and a topological order (def def-topological-order) is defined via the parent-set $\Pa^G$ (def def_3_5, item i., which is defined from $E$ alone). By item iv.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, $L_{\doit(I_W)} = L$ is transported verbatim, but neither sub-claim consults it.

\phantomsection\label{claim_3_13}

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Rem}[AcyclicHardInterventionTopologicalOrder]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg} and recall the typing and axioms of def \ref{def-cdmg}, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \ins V \times V / ((v_1, v_2) \sim (v_2, v_1))$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$). Let
    \[ W \ins J \cup V \]
    be a subset of nodes, and let
    \[ G_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; \lp J_{\doit(I_W)},\, V_{\doit(I_W)},\, E_{\doit(I_W)},\, L_{\doit(I_W)} \rp \]
    be the extended CDMG of $G$ w.r.t.\ nodes $W$ and corresponding intervention nodes $I_W$, as constructed in def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}. Both quantifiers ``for every CDMG $G$'' and ``for every $W \ins J \cup V$'' are read \emph{universally}, with the side condition $W \ins J \cup V$ inherited verbatim from def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes} (so $W$ is permitted to overlap the input-node set $J$, i.e.\ $W \cap J \neq \emptyset$ is admitted); the case $W = \emptyset$ is admitted as well (then $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC = \emptyset$ and $G_{\doit(I_W)} = G$ verbatim by items i.--iv.\ of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}).

    \emph{Notational shorthand recap from def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}.}
    Per def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, the family of intervention symbols is $I_W = \lC I_w \st w \in W \rC$ with the convention $I_j := j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ (a purely notational shorthand: no new node is introduced on the $J \cap W$ branch, the pre-existing input node $j \in J$ plays the role of its own intervention node, and the pre-existing edges incident to $j$ in $G$ are retained unchanged in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$). Consequently $I_W$ decomposes as
    \[
        I_W \;=\; (J \cap W) \;\cup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC,
    \]
    where the first component contains the (notational) $I_j = j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ and the second component contains the genuinely fresh symbols $I_w$ for $w \in W \sm J$, the latter type-disjoint from $J \cup V$ and from each other by the freshness clause of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}. We henceforth refer to the elements of $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ as the \emph{fresh intervention nodes} of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.

    \emph{Carrier of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.}
    By items i.\ and ii.\ of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes},
    \[
        J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; \lp J \cup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \rp \cup V \;=\; (J \cup V) \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC,
    \]
    where the second equality is a type-level disjoint union by the freshness clause of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes} ($\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$). In particular, $J \cup V$ is a subset of $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ via the canonical set-theoretic inclusion
    \[
        \iota \,:\, J \cup V \;\hookrightarrow\; J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}, \qquad v \;\longmapsto\; v,
    \]
    and the inclusion is strict when $W \sm J \neq \emptyset$ (the fresh intervention nodes belong to the codomain but not to the domain) and is an equality when $W \sm J = \emptyset$. In the downstream Lean rendering of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes} this inclusion is realised as the ``unsplit'' constructor of a tagged-sum carrier; the underlying set-theoretic content is plain inclusion.

    \emph{Topological orders live on different carriers.}
    By def \ref{def-topological-order}, a topological order of $G$ is a strict total order $<_G$ on $J \cup V$, while a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is a strict total order $<_{G_{\doit(I_W)}}$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. When $W \sm J \neq \emptyset$, these carriers are not equal -- $<_{G_{\doit(I_W)}}$ is a relation on the strictly larger set $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ -- so a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is not literally a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}; the bridging operations are \emph{restriction} (sub-claim~(b) below) and \emph{extension} (sub-claim~(c) below), both taken along $\iota$.

    \emph{Reading of ``extend''.}
    Following standard mathematical usage, we read ``extension of a strict total order'' in the \emph{strict} sense: a strict total order $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ \emph{extends} a strict total order $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ when its restriction to $J \cup V$ along $\iota$ recovers $<_G$, i.e.\ for every $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$,
    \[
        v_1 \,<_G\, v_2 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \iota(v_1) \,\prec\, \iota(v_2).
    \]
    This is the strict reading; the weaker reading ``some strict total order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ whose ground set contains $J \cup V$'' is too lax to constrain the construction and is not adopted here.

    \emph{The remark.} The following conjunction of three sub-claims holds.

    \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
        \item \emph{Acyclicity is preserved by adding intervention nodes.}
              If $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}, then $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}, i.e.\ for every $x \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} = (J \cup V) \dcup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$, there does not exist any non-trivial directed walk from $x$ to itself in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$. The hypothesis ``$G$ is acyclic'' is used in this sub-claim; sub-claims~(b) and~(c) below do not require acyclicity of $G$.

        \item \emph{Restriction direction: every topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ restricts to one of $G$ along $\iota$.}
              For every strict total order $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$, if $\prec$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order} (i.e.\ for every $v, w \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$, $v \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(w) \Rightarrow v \prec w$), then the binary relation $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ defined by
              \[
                  v_1 \,<_G\, v_2 \;\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \iota(v_1) \,\prec\, \iota(v_2) \qquad \text{for } v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V
              \]
              is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}. The LN's prose ``a topological order for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is also one for $G$'' is read \emph{universally} (every such $\prec$, not merely some such $\prec$) and refers to this restriction along $\iota$: the carriers differ when $W \sm J \neq \emptyset$, so an order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ cannot literally be an order on $J \cup V$, but its restriction along $\iota$ is.

        \item \emph{Extension direction: every topological order of $G$ extends to one of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.}
              For every strict total order $<_G$ on $J \cup V$, if $<_G$ is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}, then there exists a strict total order $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ such that
              \begin{itemize}
                  \item $\prec$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}; and
                  \item $\prec$ \emph{extends} $<_G$ along $\iota$ in the strict sense recorded above, i.e.\ for every $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$, $v_1 <_G v_2 \Leftrightarrow \iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_2)$.
              \end{itemize}
              The LN's prose ``any topological order of $G$ can be extended to one for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$'' is read \emph{universally} (every such $<_G$ extends) and ``extended'' is read in the strict sense.

              \emph{Example construction (the LN's ``put all $I_w$ first'').}
              A concrete witness $\prec$ for the existential of sub-claim~(c) is the following ``fresh intervention nodes first'' relation: for $x, y \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} = (J \cup V) \dcup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$,
              \[
                  x \,\prec\, y \;\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
                  \begin{cases}
                      w_1 \,<_G\, w_2 & \text{if } x = I_{w_1} \text{ and } y = I_{w_2} \text{ for some } w_1, w_2 \in W \sm J, \\
                      \text{true} & \text{if } x = I_{w} \text{ for some } w \in W \sm J \text{ and } y \in J \cup V, \\
                      \text{false} & \text{if } x \in J \cup V \text{ and } y = I_{w} \text{ for some } w \in W \sm J, \\
                      v_1 \,<_G\, v_2 & \text{if } x = v_1 \text{ and } y = v_2 \text{ for some } v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V.
                  \end{cases}
              \]
              The four cases exhaust the disjoint-union carrier $(J \cup V) \dcup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ and are pairwise mutually exclusive, so the right-hand side is well-defined. In the first case the right-hand side $w_1 <_G w_2$ uses that $W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$ -- both target nodes $w_1, w_2$ lie in $V \ins J \cup V$, the carrier of $<_G$. In words: all fresh intervention nodes $I_w$ (i.e.\ those with $w \in W \sm J$) precede all elements of $J \cup V$ under $\prec$; among the fresh intervention nodes, $\prec$ is the $<_G$-order of their target nodes; among the elements of $J \cup V$, $\prec$ is $<_G$ itself. The restriction of $\prec$ to $J \cup V$ along $\iota$ is the fourth case, which is $<_G$ verbatim, so the strict extension property is satisfied by construction.
    \end{enumerate}

    \emph{What ``putting all the $I_w$ nodes first'' refers to (resolving the literal reading of the LN's prose).}
    By the notational shorthand $I_j := j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ recalled above from def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, the literal LN phrase ``putting all the $I_w$ nodes first'' must be read as ``putting all the \emph{fresh} intervention nodes first'', i.e.\ only the $I_w$ with $w \in W \sm J$ are placed at the front of the ordering; the $I_j = j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ are not separate nodes, they coincide with pre-existing input nodes of $G$, and they retain their $<_G$-position inside the $J \cup V$-block of $\prec$. This is the only reading consistent with the strict ``extends'' property of the previous paragraph: were the $J \cap W$ nodes also moved to the front, then the restriction of $\prec$ to $J \cup V$ along $\iota$ would in general differ from $<_G$ (it would reshuffle the $J \cap W$ nodes), violating the strict extension condition.

    \emph{Order among the fresh intervention nodes is a design freedom.}
    The LN's prose does not specify the relative order \emph{among} the fresh intervention nodes $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$. Any choice that makes $\prec$ a strict total order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ and respects the parent-precedence requirement of def \ref{def-topological-order} for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is admissible. The example construction above selects the specific choice ``$I_{w_1} \prec I_{w_2} \Leftrightarrow w_1 <_G w_2$'' (using that $W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$, so $<_G$ is defined on the targets); but any strict total order on $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ would equally well discharge the topological-order requirement for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, because the fresh intervention nodes are pairwise non-adjacent in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$: each fresh $I_w$ has the unique outgoing edge $(I_w, w) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ pointing to its target $w \in V$, has no incoming edges in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (by the freshness clause of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, no element of $E_{\doit(I_W)}$ has a fresh $I_w$ as its target), and the set $\lC (I_w, w) \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ contains no pair $(I_{w_1}, I_{w_2})$ with both endpoints fresh. Sub-claim~(c) asserts \emph{existence} of an extension; the example construction above provides one explicit witness.

    \emph{Role of $L$.}
    The bidirected-edge set $L$ plays no role in any of the three sub-claims: acyclicity (def \ref{def-acylic}) is defined via the non-existence of non-trivial directed walks (which use only $E$), and a topological order (def \ref{def-topological-order}) is defined via the parent-set $\Pa^G$ (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i., which is defined from $E$ alone). By item iv.\ of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, $L_{\doit(I_W)} = L$ is transported verbatim, but neither sub-claim consults it.
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
% The LN's claim_3_13 block in lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex:822--829
% is a \begin{Rem} that only states the conjunction of three sub-claims and
% does NOT contain a \begin{proof} block (the next item, claim_3_14, begins at
% line 831 with no intervening proof). We therefore construct the proof from
% scratch, in the LN paradigm, mirroring the structure of \refrow{claim_3_9}'s
% proof (the direct architectural analog for the extending-via-intervention-nodes
% construction of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, which also faces a
% carrier change from $J \cup V$ to the disjoint union
% $(J \cup V) \dcup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$).
%
% Strategy: prove (c) first via direct verification of the four conjuncts of
% def \ref{def-topological-order} on the explicit ``fresh intervention nodes
% first'' relation $\prec$ supplied in the statement's ``Example construction''
% paragraph; prove (b) next by transport along $\iota$ (no acyclicity hypothesis
% needed); derive (a) as a one-paragraph corollary of (c) via
% \refrow{claim_3_2} (the equivalence of acyclicity and the existence of a
% topological order).

We prove the three sub-claims in the order (c), (b), then (a). Sub-claim~(a) is derived as a one-paragraph corollary of sub-claim~(c) via \refrow{claim_3_2} (the equivalence of acyclicity and the existence of a topological order, i.e.\ the \emph{acyclic-iff-topological-order} equivalence), so we settle the substantive content -- (c) and (b) -- first.

Throughout the proof we use the explicit form of the carrier and edge set of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ recalled in the statement block above, namely
\[
    J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; (J \cup V) \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC
\]
(items i.\ and ii.\ of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, type-level disjoint by the freshness clause of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$), and
\[
    E_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; \lC \lp \iota(v_1), \iota(v_2) \rp \st (v_1, v_2) \in E \rC \;\cup\; \lC \lp I_w, w \rp \st w \in W \sm J \rC
\]
(item iii.\ of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, the two edge-set-builders: \emph{lifted edges} along $\iota$ and \emph{fresh intervention edges} from each fresh $I_w$ to its target $w \in V$). The canonical inclusion $\iota : J \cup V \hookrightarrow J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$, $v \mapsto v$, is injective by construction (it is the identity on the $J \cup V$ component of the disjoint-union carrier). The bidirected-edge set $L_{\doit(I_W)} = L$ (item iv.\ of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}) plays no role in any sub-claim, for the reason recorded in the closing paragraph of the statement block above (acyclicity, def \ref{def-acylic}, and topological orders, def \ref{def-topological-order}, are both defined from $E$ alone, via the parent-set $\Pa^G$ of def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i.).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Sub-proof of (c): every topological order of $G$ extends to one of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.}

Let $<_G$ be any strict total order on $J \cup V$ that is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}; concretely, $<_G$ is a binary relation on $J \cup V$ that is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous, and satisfies the parent-precedence clause
\[
    \forall\, v, w \in J \cup V :\quad v \in \Pa^G(w) \;\Longrightarrow\; v <_G w.
\]
We exhibit the witness $\prec$ promised by the existential of sub-claim~(c) directly: take $\prec$ to be the ``fresh intervention nodes first'' relation defined in the ``Example construction'' paragraph of the statement block above, i.e.\ for $x, y \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} = (J \cup V) \dcup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$,
\[
    x \,\prec\, y \;\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
    \begin{cases}
        w_1 \,<_G\, w_2 & \text{(case 1)}\quad \text{if } x = I_{w_1},\ y = I_{w_2},\ w_1, w_2 \in W \sm J, \\
        \text{true}      & \text{(case 2)}\quad \text{if } x = I_w,\ w \in W \sm J,\ y \in J \cup V, \\
        \text{false}     & \text{(case 3)}\quad \text{if } x \in J \cup V,\ y = I_w,\ w \in W \sm J, \\
        v_1 \,<_G\, v_2  & \text{(case 4)}\quad \text{if } x = v_1,\ y = v_2,\ v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V.
    \end{cases}
\]
The four cases exhaust the disjoint-union carrier and are pairwise mutually exclusive (cf.\ the ``Example construction'' paragraph of the statement block), so $\prec$ is well-defined on the carrier of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$. The right-hand sides of cases~1 and~4 are well-defined because $W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$ (the freshness clause of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}), so $<_G$ -- a relation on $J \cup V$ -- is defined on every operand appearing on the right-hand side. We verify, in turn, each of the four conjuncts of def \ref{def-topological-order} for $\prec$ (irreflexivity, transitivity, trichotomy, parent-precedence), plus the strict-extension property along $\iota$.

For brevity in the case analyses below, we write $A$ for the constituent ``$J \cup V$ via $\iota$'' (an ``original'' node) and $B$ for the constituent ``$\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$'' (a ``fresh'' intervention node); every $x \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ lies in exactly one of $A$ or $B$, by the disjoint-union carrier identity recalled above.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Irreflexivity of $\prec$.}
Let $x \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. Two cases on the disjoint-union carrier.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Case $x \in A$, i.e.\ $x \in J \cup V$.} The pair $(x, x)$ lands in case~4 of $\prec$, so $x \prec x \Leftrightarrow x <_G x$. Irreflexivity of $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ gives $\neg(x <_G x)$, hence $\neg(x \prec x)$.
    \item \emph{Case $x \in B$, i.e.\ $x = I_w$ for some $w \in W \sm J$.} The pair $(x, x) = (I_w, I_w)$ lands in case~1 of $\prec$, so $x \prec x \Leftrightarrow w <_G w$. Irreflexivity of $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ applied at $w$ (which lies in $J \cup V$ by $W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$) gives $\neg(w <_G w)$, hence $\neg(x \prec x)$.
\end{itemize}
In either case $x \prec x$ is false, so $\prec$ is irreflexive on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Transitivity of $\prec$.}
Let $x, y, z \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ with $x \prec y$ and $y \prec z$; we show $x \prec z$. We case-split on the membership of each of $x$, $y$, $z$ in $A$ vs.\ $B$, giving eight sub-cases enumerated by the binary triple. We handle each sub-case by reading off the case of $\prec$ at each of $(x, y)$, $(y, z)$, $(x, z)$ and reducing to a single transitivity-of-$<_G$ step on $J \cup V$ or to a vacuous instance.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Triple $(A, A, A)$.} The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case~4 of $\prec$, giving $x <_G y$ on $J \cup V$; the pair $(y, z)$ also lands in case~4, giving $y <_G z$. By transitivity of $<_G$ on $J \cup V$, $x <_G z$. The pair $(x, z)$ has both endpoints in $A$, hence lands in case~4 of $\prec$, so $x \prec z$.
    \item \emph{Triple $(A, A, B)$.} The pair $(y, z)$ has $y \in A$ and $z \in B$, hence lands in case~3 of $\prec$, so $y \prec z$ evaluates to false, contradicting the hypothesis $y \prec z$. The transitivity claim holds vacuously.
    \item \emph{Triple $(A, B, A)$.} The pair $(x, y)$ has $x \in A$ and $y \in B$, hence lands in case~3 of $\prec$, so $x \prec y$ evaluates to false, contradicting the hypothesis $x \prec y$. Vacuous.
    \item \emph{Triple $(A, B, B)$.} The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case~3 of $\prec$, so $x \prec y$ evaluates to false. Vacuous.
    \item \emph{Triple $(B, A, A)$.} The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case~2 of $\prec$ (so $x \prec y$ is true regardless of the underlying $<_G$); the pair $(y, z)$ lands in case~4, giving $y <_G z$. The pair $(x, z)$ has $x \in B$ and $z \in A$, hence lands in case~2 of $\prec$, which evaluates to true; so $x \prec z$.
    \item \emph{Triple $(B, A, B)$.} The pair $(y, z)$ has $y \in A$ and $z \in B$, hence lands in case~3 of $\prec$, so $y \prec z$ evaluates to false, contradicting the hypothesis. Vacuous.
    \item \emph{Triple $(B, B, A)$.} Write $x = I_{w_x}$ and $y = I_{w_y}$ with $w_x, w_y \in W \sm J$. The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case~1 of $\prec$, giving $w_x <_G w_y$; the pair $(y, z)$ lands in case~2 (so $y \prec z$ is true). The pair $(x, z)$ has $x \in B$ and $z \in A$, hence lands in case~2 of $\prec$, which is true; so $x \prec z$.
    \item \emph{Triple $(B, B, B)$.} Write $x = I_{w_x}$, $y = I_{w_y}$, $z = I_{w_z}$ with $w_x, w_y, w_z \in W \sm J$. The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case~1, giving $w_x <_G w_y$; the pair $(y, z)$ lands in case~1, giving $w_y <_G w_z$. By transitivity of $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ (applied to $w_x, w_y, w_z \in W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$), $w_x <_G w_z$. The pair $(x, z)$ has both endpoints in $B$, hence lands in case~1 of $\prec$, so $x \prec z$.
\end{itemize}
In each of the eight sub-cases the conclusion $x \prec z$ holds (or the hypothesis is vacuous), so $\prec$ is transitive on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Trichotomy of $\prec$.}
Let $x, y \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. We case-split on the membership of each of $x$, $y$ in $A$ vs.\ $B$, giving four sub-cases. In each sub-case we show that exactly one of $x \prec y$, $x = y$, $y \prec x$ holds.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Pair $(A, A)$.} The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case~4 of $\prec$, so $x \prec y \Leftrightarrow x <_G y$; symmetrically $y \prec x \Leftrightarrow y <_G x$. Trichotomy of $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ applied to $x, y \in J \cup V$ gives exactly one of $x <_G y$, $x = y$, $y <_G x$; these read as $x \prec y$, $x = y$, $y \prec x$ respectively.
    \item \emph{Pair $(A, B)$.} Write $y = I_w$ with $w \in W \sm J$. The pair $(x, y)$ has $x \in A$ and $y \in B$, hence lands in case~3 of $\prec$, so $x \prec y$ evaluates to false. The pair $(y, x)$ has $y \in B$ and $x \in A$, hence lands in case~2, so $y \prec x$ evaluates to true. Moreover $x = y$ fails: the freshness clause of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes} gives $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$, so $x \in J \cup V$ and $y = I_w$ with $w \in W \sm J$ cannot coincide. Hence exactly one of $x \prec y$, $x = y$, $y \prec x$ holds, namely the third.
    \item \emph{Pair $(B, A)$.} Symmetric to the previous case: $x \prec y$ evaluates to true (case~2), $y \prec x$ evaluates to false (case~3), and $x = y$ fails by the freshness clause as above. The first alternative is the one that holds.
    \item \emph{Pair $(B, B)$.} Write $x = I_{w_x}$ and $y = I_{w_y}$ with $w_x, w_y \in W \sm J$. The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case~1 of $\prec$, so $x \prec y \Leftrightarrow w_x <_G w_y$; symmetrically $y \prec x \Leftrightarrow w_y <_G w_x$. Trichotomy of $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ applied to $w_x, w_y \in W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$ gives exactly one of $w_x <_G w_y$, $w_x = w_y$, $w_y <_G w_x$. The first reads as $x \prec y$; the third as $y \prec x$; the middle, $w_x = w_y$, gives $x = I_{w_x} = I_{w_y} = y$ by injectivity of the assignment $w \mapsto I_w$ on $W \sm J$ (recorded in the freshness clause of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}: the fresh symbols are pairwise distinct).
\end{itemize}
In each of the four sub-cases, exactly one of $x \prec y$, $x = y$, $y \prec x$ holds, so $\prec$ is trichotomous on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.

\smallskip
Combining irreflexivity, transitivity, and trichotomy, $\prec$ is a strict total order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Parent-precedence of $\prec$ w.r.t.\ $E_{\doit(I_W)}$.}
Let $x, y \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ with $x \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(y)$. By the set-builder definition of the parent set (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i.) applied to the CDMG $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, this is equivalent to $(x, y) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$. By the explicit form of $E_{\doit(I_W)}$ recalled at the top of the proof (item iii.\ of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}), exactly one of two sub-cases holds (and by the freshness clause of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes} the two set-builders are disjoint, since every pair produced by the second set-builder has its first component $I_w$ with $w \in W \sm J$, which is type-disjoint from $J \cup V$ via $\iota$).
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Lifted edge.} $(x, y) = (\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2))$ for some $(v_1, v_2) \in E$. Then $x = \iota(v_1) \in A$ and $y = \iota(v_2) \in A$, so the pair $(x, y)$ lands in case~4 of $\prec$, giving $x \prec y \Leftrightarrow v_1 <_G v_2$. Since $(v_1, v_2) \in E$, the set-builder definition of $\Pa^G$ (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i.) gives $v_1 \in \Pa^G(v_2)$. The hypothesis ``$<_G$ is a topological order of $G$'' (def \ref{def-topological-order}), applied to $v_1 \in \Pa^G(v_2)$, yields $v_1 <_G v_2$, hence $x \prec y$.
    \item \emph{Fresh intervention edge.} $(x, y) = (I_w, w)$ for some $w \in W \sm J$. Then $x = I_w \in B$ (a fresh intervention node) and $y = w \in V \ins J \cup V$, i.e.\ $y \in A$ via $\iota$; the freshness clause of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes} gives $W \sm J \ins V$, so $y$ is a well-defined element of the $A$-side of the carrier. The pair $(x, y) = (I_w, w)$ lands in case~2 of $\prec$ ($x \in B$ with $x = I_w$, $w \in W \sm J$; $y \in J \cup V$), which evaluates to true; hence $x \prec y$.
\end{itemize}
In either sub-case, $x \prec y$ holds, so the parent-precedence clause of def \ref{def-topological-order} is verified for $\prec$ on $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.

\smallskip
Combining the strict-total-order check (irreflexivity, transitivity, trichotomy) and the parent-precedence check, $\prec$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Strict extension along $\iota$.}
For every $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$, the pair $(\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2))$ has both endpoints in $A$, hence lands in case~4 of $\prec$:
\[
    \iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_2) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_1 <_G v_2.
\]
This is the strict-extension property of the ``Reading of extend'' paragraph in the statement block above, verbatim: the restriction of $\prec$ to $J \cup V$ along $\iota$ recovers $<_G$. The witness $\prec$ exhibited above is therefore a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ that extends $<_G$ along $\iota$ in the strict sense, establishing the existential of sub-claim~(c).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Sub-proof of (b): every topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ restricts to one of $G$ along $\iota$.}

Let $\prec$ be any strict total order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ that is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}; concretely, $\prec$ is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$, and satisfies the parent-precedence clause
\[
    \forall\, x, y \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} :\quad x \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(y) \;\Longrightarrow\; x \prec y.
\]
Define the binary relation $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ by
\[
    v_1 \,<_G\, v_2 \;\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \iota(v_1) \,\prec\, \iota(v_2) \qquad \text{for } v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V.
\]
We verify each of the four conjuncts of def \ref{def-topological-order} for $<_G$ on the CDMG $G$.

We make repeated use of the elementary \emph{membership lift along $\iota$}: for every $v \in J \cup V$, $\iota(v) \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ directly, by the carrier identity
\[
    J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; (J \cup V) \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \;\supseteq\; J \cup V \;\ni\; v
\]
recalled at the top of the proof (items i.\ and ii.\ of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}).

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Irreflexivity of $<_G$.}
Let $v \in J \cup V$. By the membership lift, $\iota(v) \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. Irreflexivity of $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ applied at $\iota(v)$ yields $\neg(\iota(v) \prec \iota(v))$, i.e.\ $\neg(v <_G v)$ by the definition of $<_G$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Transitivity of $<_G$.}
Let $v_1, v_2, v_3 \in J \cup V$ with $v_1 <_G v_2$ and $v_2 <_G v_3$. By the definition of $<_G$, $\iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_2)$ and $\iota(v_2) \prec \iota(v_3)$. The membership lift on each of $v_1, v_2, v_3$ places $\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2), \iota(v_3) \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$, so transitivity of $\prec$ on the larger carrier yields $\iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_3)$, i.e.\ $v_1 <_G v_3$ by the definition of $<_G$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Trichotomy of $<_G$.}
Let $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$. The membership lift places $\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2) \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. Trichotomy of $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ applied to $\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2)$ gives exactly one of
\[
    \iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_2), \quad \iota(v_1) = \iota(v_2), \quad \iota(v_2) \prec \iota(v_1).
\]
The first reads as $v_1 <_G v_2$ by the definition of $<_G$; the third as $v_2 <_G v_1$; the middle case $\iota(v_1) = \iota(v_2)$ gives $v_1 = v_2$ by injectivity of the canonical inclusion $\iota : J \cup V \hookrightarrow J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ (recorded at the top of the proof and in the statement block). Hence exactly one of $v_1 <_G v_2$, $v_1 = v_2$, $v_2 <_G v_1$ holds.

\smallskip
Combining irreflexivity, transitivity, and trichotomy, $<_G$ is a strict total order on $J \cup V$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Parent-precedence of $<_G$ w.r.t.\ $E$.}
Let $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$ with $v_1 \in \Pa^G(v_2)$. By the set-builder definition of the parent set (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i.) applied to $G$, this is $(v_1, v_2) \in E$. By the lifted-edge clause of $E_{\doit(I_W)}$ recalled at the top of the proof (item iii.\ of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, first set-builder), $(\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2)) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$. Applying the parent-set definition (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i.) to $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, this gives $\iota(v_1) \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(\iota(v_2))$ (using the membership lift to place both endpoints in $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$). The parent-precedence hypothesis on $\prec$ (a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$) applied to $\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2)$ yields $\iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_2)$, i.e.\ $v_1 <_G v_2$ by the definition of $<_G$.

\smallskip
Both conjuncts of def \ref{def-topological-order} are verified for $<_G$ on the CDMG $G$, so $<_G$ is a topological order of $G$, establishing sub-claim~(b).

Sub-claim~(b) uses no acyclicity hypothesis on $G$: the four verifications above are pure transport along $\iota$, requiring only $\iota$'s injectivity (for trichotomy) and the lifted-edge clause of $E_{\doit(I_W)}$ (for parent-precedence). This matches the corresponding observation for \refrow{claim_3_3} sub-claim~(b) (topological-order preservation under $\doit$ does not require $G$ acyclic).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Sub-proof of (a): if $G$ is acyclic, then $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is acyclic.}

Suppose $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}. By the $\Rightarrow$ direction of \refrow{claim_3_2} (the equivalence ``$G$ is acyclic iff there exists a topological order of $G$'', i.e.\ the \emph{acyclic-iff-topological-order} equivalence), applied to the acyclic CDMG $G$, there exists some strict total order $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ that is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}.

By sub-claim~(c) (just established), the ``fresh intervention nodes first'' relation $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ constructed from this $<_G$ via the four-case definition above is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}. In particular, $\prec$ witnesses the existence of a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.

By the $\Leftarrow$ direction of \refrow{claim_3_2}, applied to the CDMG $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (which is a CDMG by the consistency check of def \ref{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}, items i.--iv.), the existence of the topological order $\prec$ of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ implies that $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}. This establishes sub-claim~(a).
\end{proof}

\end{document}

Remark — AcyclicHardInterventionTopologicalOrder

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and recall the typing and axioms of def def-cdmg, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \ins V \times V / ((v_1, v_2) \sim (v_2, v_1))$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$). Let \[ W \ins J \cup V \] be a subset of nodes, and let \[ G_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; \lp J_{\doit(I_W)},\, V_{\doit(I_W)},\, E_{\doit(I_W)},\, L_{\doit(I_W)} \rp \] be the extended CDMG of $G$ w.r.t.\ nodes $W$ and corresponding intervention nodes $I_W$, as constructed in def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes. Both quantifiers “for every CDMG $G$” and “for every $W \ins J \cup V$” are read universally, with the side condition $W \ins J \cup V$ inherited verbatim from def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes (so $W$ is permitted to overlap the input-node set $J$, i.e.\ $W \cap J \neq \emptyset$ is admitted); the case $W = \emptyset$ is admitted as well (then $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC = \emptyset$ and $G_{\doit(I_W)} = G$ verbatim by items i.–iv.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes).

Notational shorthand recap from def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes. Per def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, the family of intervention symbols is $I_W = \lC I_w \st w \in W \rC$ with the convention $I_j := j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ (a purely notational shorthand: no new node is introduced on the $J \cap W$ branch, the pre-existing input node $j \in J$ plays the role of its own intervention node, and the pre-existing edges incident to $j$ in $G$ are retained unchanged in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$). Consequently $I_W$ decomposes as \[ I_W \;=\; (J \cap W) \;\cup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC, \] where the first component contains the (notational) $I_j = j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ and the second component contains the genuinely fresh symbols $I_w$ for $w \in W \sm J$, the latter type-disjoint from $J \cup V$ and from each other by the freshness clause of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes. We henceforth refer to the elements of $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ as the fresh intervention nodes of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.

Carrier of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$. By items i.\ and ii.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, \[ J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; \lp J \cup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \rp \cup V \;=\; (J \cup V) \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC, \] where the second equality is a type-level disjoint union by the freshness clause of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes ($\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$). In particular, $J \cup V$ is a subset of $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ via the canonical set-theoretic inclusion \[ \iota \,:\, J \cup V \;\hookrightarrow\; J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}, \qquad v \;\longmapsto\; v, \] and the inclusion is strict when $W \sm J \neq \emptyset$ (the fresh intervention nodes belong to the codomain but not to the domain) and is an equality when $W \sm J = \emptyset$. In the downstream Lean rendering of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes this inclusion is realised as the “unsplit” constructor of a tagged-sum carrier; the underlying set-theoretic content is plain inclusion.

Topological orders live on different carriers. By def def-topological-order, a topological order of $G$ is a strict total order $<_G$ on $J \cup V$, while a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is a strict total order $<_{G_{\doit(I_W)}}$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. When $W \sm J \neq \emptyset$, these carriers are not equal – $<_{G_{\doit(I_W)}}$ is a relation on the strictly larger set $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ – so a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is not literally a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def def-topological-order; the bridging operations are restriction (sub-claim (b) below) and extension (sub-claim (c) below), both taken along $\iota$.

Reading of “extend”. Following standard mathematical usage, we read “extension of a strict total order” in the strict sense: a strict total order $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ extends a strict total order $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ when its restriction to $J \cup V$ along $\iota$ recovers $<_G$, i.e.\ for every $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$, \[ v_1 \,<_G\, v_2 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \iota(v_1) \,\prec\, \iota(v_2). \] This is the strict reading; the weaker reading “some strict total order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ whose ground set contains $J \cup V$” is too lax to constrain the construction and is not adopted here.

The remark. The following conjunction of three sub-claims holds.

  1. Acyclicity is preserved by adding intervention nodes. If $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic, then $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic, i.e.\ for every $x \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} = (J \cup V) \dcup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$, there does not exist any non-trivial directed walk from $x$ to itself in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$. The hypothesis “$G$ is acyclic” is used in this sub-claim; sub-claims (b) and (c) below do not require acyclicity of $G$.
  2. Restriction direction: every topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ restricts to one of $G$ along $\iota$. For every strict total order $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$, if $\prec$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def def-topological-order (i.e.\ for every $v, w \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$, $v \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(w) \Rightarrow v \prec w$), then the binary relation $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ defined by \[ v_1 \,<_G\, v_2 \;\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \iota(v_1) \,\prec\, \iota(v_2) \qquad \text{for } v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V \] is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def def-topological-order. The LN's prose “a topological order for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is also one for $G$” is read universally (every such $\prec$, not merely some such $\prec$) and refers to this restriction along $\iota$: the carriers differ when $W \sm J \neq \emptyset$, so an order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ cannot literally be an order on $J \cup V$, but its restriction along $\iota$ is.
  3. Extension direction: every topological order of $G$ extends to one of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$. For every strict total order $<_G$ on $J \cup V$, if $<_G$ is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def def-topological-order, then there exists a strict total order $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ such that
    • $\prec$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def def-topological-order; and
    • $\prec$ extends $<_G$ along $\iota$ in the strict sense recorded above, i.e.\ for every $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$, $v_1 <_G v_2 \Leftrightarrow \iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_2)$.

    The LN's prose “any topological order of $G$ can be extended to one for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$” is read universally (every such $<_G$ extends) and “extended” is read in the strict sense.

    Example construction (the LN's “put all $I_w$ first”). A concrete witness $\prec$ for the existential of sub-claim (c) is the following “fresh intervention nodes first” relation: for $x, y \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} = (J \cup V) \dcup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$, \[ x \,\prec\, y \;\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \begin{cases} w_1 \,<_G\, w_2 & \text{if } x = I_{w_1} \text{ and } y = I_{w_2} \text{ for some } w_1, w_2 \in W \sm J, \\ \text{true} & \text{if } x = I_{w} \text{ for some } w \in W \sm J \text{ and } y \in J \cup V, \\ \text{false} & \text{if } x \in J \cup V \text{ and } y = I_{w} \text{ for some } w \in W \sm J, \\ v_1 \,<_G\, v_2 & \text{if } x = v_1 \text{ and } y = v_2 \text{ for some } v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V. \end{cases} \] The four cases exhaust the disjoint-union carrier $(J \cup V) \dcup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ and are pairwise mutually exclusive, so the right-hand side is well-defined. In the first case the right-hand side $w_1 <_G w_2$ uses that $W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$ – both target nodes $w_1, w_2$ lie in $V \ins J \cup V$, the carrier of $<_G$. In words: all fresh intervention nodes $I_w$ (i.e.\ those with $w \in W \sm J$) precede all elements of $J \cup V$ under $\prec$; among the fresh intervention nodes, $\prec$ is the $<_G$-order of their target nodes; among the elements of $J \cup V$, $\prec$ is $<_G$ itself. The restriction of $\prec$ to $J \cup V$ along $\iota$ is the fourth case, which is $<_G$ verbatim, so the strict extension property is satisfied by construction.

What “putting all the $I_w$ nodes first” refers to (resolving the literal reading of the LN's prose). By the notational shorthand $I_j := j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ recalled above from def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, the literal LN phrase “putting all the $I_w$ nodes first” must be read as “putting all the fresh intervention nodes first”, i.e.\ only the $I_w$ with $w \in W \sm J$ are placed at the front of the ordering; the $I_j = j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ are not separate nodes, they coincide with pre-existing input nodes of $G$, and they retain their $<_G$-position inside the $J \cup V$-block of $\prec$. This is the only reading consistent with the strict “extends” property of the previous paragraph: were the $J \cap W$ nodes also moved to the front, then the restriction of $\prec$ to $J \cup V$ along $\iota$ would in general differ from $<_G$ (it would reshuffle the $J \cap W$ nodes), violating the strict extension condition.

Order among the fresh intervention nodes is a design freedom. The LN's prose does not specify the relative order among the fresh intervention nodes $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$. Any choice that makes $\prec$ a strict total order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ and respects the parent-precedence requirement of def def-topological-order for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is admissible. The example construction above selects the specific choice “$I_{w_1} \prec I_{w_2} \Leftrightarrow w_1 <_G w_2$” (using that $W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$, so $<_G$ is defined on the targets); but any strict total order on $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ would equally well discharge the topological-order requirement for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, because the fresh intervention nodes are pairwise non-adjacent in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$: each fresh $I_w$ has the unique outgoing edge $(I_w, w) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$ pointing to its target $w \in V$, has no incoming edges in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (by the freshness clause of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, no element of $E_{\doit(I_W)}$ has a fresh $I_w$ as its target), and the set $\lC (I_w, w) \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ contains no pair $(I_{w_1}, I_{w_2})$ with both endpoints fresh. Sub-claim (c) asserts existence of an extension; the example construction above provides one explicit witness.

Role of $L$. The bidirected-edge set $L$ plays no role in any of the three sub-claims: acyclicity (def def-acylic) is defined via the non-existence of non-trivial directed walks (which use only $E$), and a topological order (def def-topological-order) is defined via the parent-set $\Pa^G$ (def def_3_5, item i., which is defined from $E$ alone). By item iv.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, $L_{\doit(I_W)} = L$ is transported verbatim, but neither sub-claim consults it.

Proof.

We prove the three sub-claims in the order (c), (b), then (a). Sub-claim (a) is derived as a one-paragraph corollary of sub-claim (c) via claim_3_2 (the equivalence of acyclicity and the existence of a topological order, i.e.\ the acyclic-iff-topological-order equivalence), so we settle the substantive content – (c) and (b) – first.

Throughout the proof we use the explicit form of the carrier and edge set of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ recalled in the statement block above, namely \[ J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; (J \cup V) \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \] (items i.\ and ii.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, type-level disjoint by the freshness clause of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$), and \[ E_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; \lC \lp \iota(v_1), \iota(v_2) \rp \st (v_1, v_2) \in E \rC \;\cup\; \lC \lp I_w, w \rp \st w \in W \sm J \rC \] (item iii.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, the two edge-set-builders: lifted edges along $\iota$ and fresh intervention edges from each fresh $I_w$ to its target $w \in V$). The canonical inclusion $\iota : J \cup V \hookrightarrow J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$, $v \mapsto v$, is injective by construction (it is the identity on the $J \cup V$ component of the disjoint-union carrier). The bidirected-edge set $L_{\doit(I_W)} = L$ (item iv.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes) plays no role in any sub-claim, for the reason recorded in the closing paragraph of the statement block above (acyclicity, def def-acylic, and topological orders, def def-topological-order, are both defined from $E$ alone, via the parent-set $\Pa^G$ of def def_3_5, item i.).

Sub-proof of (c): every topological order of $G$ extends to one of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.

Let $<_G$ be any strict total order on $J \cup V$ that is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def def-topological-order; concretely, $<_G$ is a binary relation on $J \cup V$ that is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous, and satisfies the parent-precedence clause \[ \forall\, v, w \in J \cup V :\quad v \in \Pa^G(w) \;\Longrightarrow\; v <_G w. \] We exhibit the witness $\prec$ promised by the existential of sub-claim (c) directly: take $\prec$ to be the “fresh intervention nodes first” relation defined in the “Example construction” paragraph of the statement block above, i.e.\ for $x, y \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} = (J \cup V) \dcup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$, \[ x \,\prec\, y \;\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \begin{cases} w_1 \,<_G\, w_2 & \text{(case 1)}\quad \text{if } x = I_{w_1},\ y = I_{w_2},\ w_1, w_2 \in W \sm J, \\ \text{true} & \text{(case 2)}\quad \text{if } x = I_w,\ w \in W \sm J,\ y \in J \cup V, \\ \text{false} & \text{(case 3)}\quad \text{if } x \in J \cup V,\ y = I_w,\ w \in W \sm J, \\ v_1 \,<_G\, v_2 & \text{(case 4)}\quad \text{if } x = v_1,\ y = v_2,\ v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V. \end{cases} \] The four cases exhaust the disjoint-union carrier and are pairwise mutually exclusive (cf.\ the “Example construction” paragraph of the statement block), so $\prec$ is well-defined on the carrier of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$. The right-hand sides of cases 1 and 4 are well-defined because $W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$ (the freshness clause of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes), so $<_G$ – a relation on $J \cup V$ – is defined on every operand appearing on the right-hand side. We verify, in turn, each of the four conjuncts of def def-topological-order for $\prec$ (irreflexivity, transitivity, trichotomy, parent-precedence), plus the strict-extension property along $\iota$.

For brevity in the case analyses below, we write $A$ for the constituent “$J \cup V$ via $\iota$” (an “original” node) and $B$ for the constituent “$\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$” (a “fresh” intervention node); every $x \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ lies in exactly one of $A$ or $B$, by the disjoint-union carrier identity recalled above.

Irreflexivity of $\prec$. Let $x \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. Two cases on the disjoint-union carrier.

  • Case $x \in A$, i.e.\ $x \in J \cup V$. The pair $(x, x)$ lands in case 4 of $\prec$, so $x \prec x \Leftrightarrow x <_G x$. Irreflexivity of $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ gives $\neg(x <_G x)$, hence $\neg(x \prec x)$.
  • Case $x \in B$, i.e.\ $x = I_w$ for some $w \in W \sm J$. The pair $(x, x) = (I_w, I_w)$ lands in case 1 of $\prec$, so $x \prec x \Leftrightarrow w <_G w$. Irreflexivity of $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ applied at $w$ (which lies in $J \cup V$ by $W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$) gives $\neg(w <_G w)$, hence $\neg(x \prec x)$.

In either case $x \prec x$ is false, so $\prec$ is irreflexive on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.

Transitivity of $\prec$. Let $x, y, z \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ with $x \prec y$ and $y \prec z$; we show $x \prec z$. We case-split on the membership of each of $x$, $y$, $z$ in $A$ vs.\ $B$, giving eight sub-cases enumerated by the binary triple. We handle each sub-case by reading off the case of $\prec$ at each of $(x, y)$, $(y, z)$, $(x, z)$ and reducing to a single transitivity-of-$<_G$ step on $J \cup V$ or to a vacuous instance.

  • Triple $(A, A, A)$. The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case 4 of $\prec$, giving $x <_G y$ on $J \cup V$; the pair $(y, z)$ also lands in case 4, giving $y <_G z$. By transitivity of $<_G$ on $J \cup V$, $x <_G z$. The pair $(x, z)$ has both endpoints in $A$, hence lands in case 4 of $\prec$, so $x \prec z$.
  • Triple $(A, A, B)$. The pair $(y, z)$ has $y \in A$ and $z \in B$, hence lands in case 3 of $\prec$, so $y \prec z$ evaluates to false, contradicting the hypothesis $y \prec z$. The transitivity claim holds vacuously.
  • Triple $(A, B, A)$. The pair $(x, y)$ has $x \in A$ and $y \in B$, hence lands in case 3 of $\prec$, so $x \prec y$ evaluates to false, contradicting the hypothesis $x \prec y$. Vacuous.
  • Triple $(A, B, B)$. The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case 3 of $\prec$, so $x \prec y$ evaluates to false. Vacuous.
  • Triple $(B, A, A)$. The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case 2 of $\prec$ (so $x \prec y$ is true regardless of the underlying $<_G$); the pair $(y, z)$ lands in case 4, giving $y <_G z$. The pair $(x, z)$ has $x \in B$ and $z \in A$, hence lands in case 2 of $\prec$, which evaluates to true; so $x \prec z$.
  • Triple $(B, A, B)$. The pair $(y, z)$ has $y \in A$ and $z \in B$, hence lands in case 3 of $\prec$, so $y \prec z$ evaluates to false, contradicting the hypothesis. Vacuous.
  • Triple $(B, B, A)$. Write $x = I_{w_x}$ and $y = I_{w_y}$ with $w_x, w_y \in W \sm J$. The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case 1 of $\prec$, giving $w_x <_G w_y$; the pair $(y, z)$ lands in case 2 (so $y \prec z$ is true). The pair $(x, z)$ has $x \in B$ and $z \in A$, hence lands in case 2 of $\prec$, which is true; so $x \prec z$.
  • Triple $(B, B, B)$. Write $x = I_{w_x}$, $y = I_{w_y}$, $z = I_{w_z}$ with $w_x, w_y, w_z \in W \sm J$. The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case 1, giving $w_x <_G w_y$; the pair $(y, z)$ lands in case 1, giving $w_y <_G w_z$. By transitivity of $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ (applied to $w_x, w_y, w_z \in W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$), $w_x <_G w_z$. The pair $(x, z)$ has both endpoints in $B$, hence lands in case 1 of $\prec$, so $x \prec z$.

In each of the eight sub-cases the conclusion $x \prec z$ holds (or the hypothesis is vacuous), so $\prec$ is transitive on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.

Trichotomy of $\prec$. Let $x, y \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. We case-split on the membership of each of $x$, $y$ in $A$ vs.\ $B$, giving four sub-cases. In each sub-case we show that exactly one of $x \prec y$, $x = y$, $y \prec x$ holds.

  • Pair $(A, A)$. The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case 4 of $\prec$, so $x \prec y \Leftrightarrow x <_G y$; symmetrically $y \prec x \Leftrightarrow y <_G x$. Trichotomy of $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ applied to $x, y \in J \cup V$ gives exactly one of $x <_G y$, $x = y$, $y <_G x$; these read as $x \prec y$, $x = y$, $y \prec x$ respectively.
  • Pair $(A, B)$. Write $y = I_w$ with $w \in W \sm J$. The pair $(x, y)$ has $x \in A$ and $y \in B$, hence lands in case 3 of $\prec$, so $x \prec y$ evaluates to false. The pair $(y, x)$ has $y \in B$ and $x \in A$, hence lands in case 2, so $y \prec x$ evaluates to true. Moreover $x = y$ fails: the freshness clause of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes gives $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$, so $x \in J \cup V$ and $y = I_w$ with $w \in W \sm J$ cannot coincide. Hence exactly one of $x \prec y$, $x = y$, $y \prec x$ holds, namely the third.
  • Pair $(B, A)$. Symmetric to the previous case: $x \prec y$ evaluates to true (case 2), $y \prec x$ evaluates to false (case 3), and $x = y$ fails by the freshness clause as above. The first alternative is the one that holds.
  • Pair $(B, B)$. Write $x = I_{w_x}$ and $y = I_{w_y}$ with $w_x, w_y \in W \sm J$. The pair $(x, y)$ lands in case 1 of $\prec$, so $x \prec y \Leftrightarrow w_x <_G w_y$; symmetrically $y \prec x \Leftrightarrow w_y <_G w_x$. Trichotomy of $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ applied to $w_x, w_y \in W \sm J \ins V \ins J \cup V$ gives exactly one of $w_x <_G w_y$, $w_x = w_y$, $w_y <_G w_x$. The first reads as $x \prec y$; the third as $y \prec x$; the middle, $w_x = w_y$, gives $x = I_{w_x} = I_{w_y} = y$ by injectivity of the assignment $w \mapsto I_w$ on $W \sm J$ (recorded in the freshness clause of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes: the fresh symbols are pairwise distinct).

In each of the four sub-cases, exactly one of $x \prec y$, $x = y$, $y \prec x$ holds, so $\prec$ is trichotomous on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.

Combining irreflexivity, transitivity, and trichotomy, $\prec$ is a strict total order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$.

Parent-precedence of $\prec$ w.r.t.\ $E_{\doit(I_W)}$. Let $x, y \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ with $x \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(y)$. By the set-builder definition of the parent set (def def_3_5, item i.) applied to the CDMG $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, this is equivalent to $(x, y) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$. By the explicit form of $E_{\doit(I_W)}$ recalled at the top of the proof (item iii.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes), exactly one of two sub-cases holds (and by the freshness clause of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes the two set-builders are disjoint, since every pair produced by the second set-builder has its first component $I_w$ with $w \in W \sm J$, which is type-disjoint from $J \cup V$ via $\iota$).

  • Lifted edge. $(x, y) = (\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2))$ for some $(v_1, v_2) \in E$. Then $x = \iota(v_1) \in A$ and $y = \iota(v_2) \in A$, so the pair $(x, y)$ lands in case 4 of $\prec$, giving $x \prec y \Leftrightarrow v_1 <_G v_2$. Since $(v_1, v_2) \in E$, the set-builder definition of $\Pa^G$ (def def_3_5, item i.) gives $v_1 \in \Pa^G(v_2)$. The hypothesis “$<_G$ is a topological order of $G$” (def def-topological-order), applied to $v_1 \in \Pa^G(v_2)$, yields $v_1 <_G v_2$, hence $x \prec y$.
  • Fresh intervention edge. $(x, y) = (I_w, w)$ for some $w \in W \sm J$. Then $x = I_w \in B$ (a fresh intervention node) and $y = w \in V \ins J \cup V$, i.e.\ $y \in A$ via $\iota$; the freshness clause of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes gives $W \sm J \ins V$, so $y$ is a well-defined element of the $A$-side of the carrier. The pair $(x, y) = (I_w, w)$ lands in case 2 of $\prec$ ($x \in B$ with $x = I_w$, $w \in W \sm J$; $y \in J \cup V$), which evaluates to true; hence $x \prec y$.

In either sub-case, $x \prec y$ holds, so the parent-precedence clause of def def-topological-order is verified for $\prec$ on $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.

Combining the strict-total-order check (irreflexivity, transitivity, trichotomy) and the parent-precedence check, $\prec$ is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def def-topological-order.

Strict extension along $\iota$. For every $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$, the pair $(\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2))$ has both endpoints in $A$, hence lands in case 4 of $\prec$: \[ \iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_2) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_1 <_G v_2. \] This is the strict-extension property of the “Reading of extend” paragraph in the statement block above, verbatim: the restriction of $\prec$ to $J \cup V$ along $\iota$ recovers $<_G$. The witness $\prec$ exhibited above is therefore a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ that extends $<_G$ along $\iota$ in the strict sense, establishing the existential of sub-claim (c).

Sub-proof of (b): every topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ restricts to one of $G$ along $\iota$.

Let $\prec$ be any strict total order on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ that is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def def-topological-order; concretely, $\prec$ is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$, and satisfies the parent-precedence clause \[ \forall\, x, y \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} :\quad x \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(y) \;\Longrightarrow\; x \prec y. \] Define the binary relation $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ by \[ v_1 \,<_G\, v_2 \;\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \iota(v_1) \,\prec\, \iota(v_2) \qquad \text{for } v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V. \] We verify each of the four conjuncts of def def-topological-order for $<_G$ on the CDMG $G$.

We make repeated use of the elementary membership lift along $\iota$: for every $v \in J \cup V$, $\iota(v) \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ directly, by the carrier identity \[ J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)} \;=\; (J \cup V) \;\dcup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \;\supseteq\; J \cup V \;\ni\; v \] recalled at the top of the proof (items i.\ and ii.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes).

Irreflexivity of $<_G$. Let $v \in J \cup V$. By the membership lift, $\iota(v) \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. Irreflexivity of $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ applied at $\iota(v)$ yields $\neg(\iota(v) \prec \iota(v))$, i.e.\ $\neg(v <_G v)$ by the definition of $<_G$.

Transitivity of $<_G$. Let $v_1, v_2, v_3 \in J \cup V$ with $v_1 <_G v_2$ and $v_2 <_G v_3$. By the definition of $<_G$, $\iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_2)$ and $\iota(v_2) \prec \iota(v_3)$. The membership lift on each of $v_1, v_2, v_3$ places $\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2), \iota(v_3) \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$, so transitivity of $\prec$ on the larger carrier yields $\iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_3)$, i.e.\ $v_1 <_G v_3$ by the definition of $<_G$.

Trichotomy of $<_G$. Let $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$. The membership lift places $\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2) \in J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$. Trichotomy of $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ applied to $\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2)$ gives exactly one of \[ \iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_2), \quad \iota(v_1) = \iota(v_2), \quad \iota(v_2) \prec \iota(v_1). \] The first reads as $v_1 <_G v_2$ by the definition of $<_G$; the third as $v_2 <_G v_1$; the middle case $\iota(v_1) = \iota(v_2)$ gives $v_1 = v_2$ by injectivity of the canonical inclusion $\iota : J \cup V \hookrightarrow J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ (recorded at the top of the proof and in the statement block). Hence exactly one of $v_1 <_G v_2$, $v_1 = v_2$, $v_2 <_G v_1$ holds.

Combining irreflexivity, transitivity, and trichotomy, $<_G$ is a strict total order on $J \cup V$.

Parent-precedence of $<_G$ w.r.t.\ $E$. Let $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$ with $v_1 \in \Pa^G(v_2)$. By the set-builder definition of the parent set (def def_3_5, item i.) applied to $G$, this is $(v_1, v_2) \in E$. By the lifted-edge clause of $E_{\doit(I_W)}$ recalled at the top of the proof (item iii.\ of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, first set-builder), $(\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2)) \in E_{\doit(I_W)}$. Applying the parent-set definition (def def_3_5, item i.) to $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, this gives $\iota(v_1) \in \Pa^{G_{\doit(I_W)}}(\iota(v_2))$ (using the membership lift to place both endpoints in $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$). The parent-precedence hypothesis on $\prec$ (a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$) applied to $\iota(v_1), \iota(v_2)$ yields $\iota(v_1) \prec \iota(v_2)$, i.e.\ $v_1 <_G v_2$ by the definition of $<_G$.

Both conjuncts of def def-topological-order are verified for $<_G$ on the CDMG $G$, so $<_G$ is a topological order of $G$, establishing sub-claim (b).

Sub-claim (b) uses no acyclicity hypothesis on $G$: the four verifications above are pure transport along $\iota$, requiring only $\iota$'s injectivity (for trichotomy) and the lifted-edge clause of $E_{\doit(I_W)}$ (for parent-precedence). This matches the corresponding observation for claim_3_3 sub-claim (b) (topological-order preservation under $\doit$ does not require $G$ acyclic).

Sub-proof of (a): if $G$ is acyclic, then $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is acyclic.

Suppose $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic. By the $\Rightarrow$ direction of claim_3_2 (the equivalence “$G$ is acyclic iff there exists a topological order of $G$”, i.e.\ the acyclic-iff-topological-order equivalence), applied to the acyclic CDMG $G$, there exists some strict total order $<_G$ on $J \cup V$ that is a topological order of $G$ in the sense of def def-topological-order.

By sub-claim (c) (just established), the “fresh intervention nodes first” relation $\prec$ on $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ constructed from this $<_G$ via the four-case definition above is a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ in the sense of def def-topological-order. In particular, $\prec$ witnesses the existence of a topological order of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$.

By the $\Leftarrow$ direction of claim_3_2, applied to the CDMG $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ (which is a CDMG by the consistency check of def def:cdmg_intervention_nodes, items i.–iv.), the existence of the topological order $\prec$ of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ implies that $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic. This establishes sub-claim (a).

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `extOrder`, `restrictOrder`, and the three main theorems. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing because every signature in this row threads through `CDMG Node` (which itself requires `DecidableEq Node` per `def_3_1`) and through `G.extendingCDMGsWith W hW` (`def_3_13`), whose tagged-sum carrier `IntExtNode Node` derives its own `DecidableEq` instance from this one — the derived instance is in turn consumed by the `Pa : IntExtNode Node → Set (IntExtNode Node)` projection used in `G.IsTopologicalOrder` (`def_3_8`) and by the `Finset.image` / `Finset.filter` calls that build the extension's edge sets. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g. `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.

-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability is inherited by `IntExtNode Node` and drives the `Finset.image`/`filter` calls in `extendingCDMGsWith`.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

def extOrder (lt : Node → Node → Prop) :
    IntExtNode Node → IntExtNode Node → Prop
  | .intCopy w1, .intCopy w2 => lt w1 w2
  | .intCopy _,  .unsplit _  => True
  | .unsplit _,  .intCopy _  => False
  | .unsplit u1, .unsplit u2 => lt u1 u2
lean_blocks.explanation: This helper lifts a strict relation `lt : Node → Node → Prop` on the original carrier to a strict relation on the extended carrier `IntExtNode Node` (from `def_3_13`), realising the LN's `put all $I_w$ first` example construction at the type level. The four cases pattern-match on the two `IntExtNode` constructors `.intCopy` (fresh intervention nodes $I_w$ for $w \in W \setminus J$) and `.unsplit` (the canonical inclusion $\iota$ of original nodes $J \cup V$): every `.intCopy` precedes every `.unsplit` (cases two and three pin this lex orientation with `True` / `False`), and within each tag class the order delegates to `lt` on the underlying base nodes. The orientation `.intCopy` below `.unsplit` is forced by parent precedence — every fresh edge added by `extendingCDMGsWith` has shape `(.intCopy w, .unsplit w)`, so the topological-order proof of `extExtendsTopologicalOrder` reads off the `True` clause directly. `W` is absent from the signature: the `W`-dependence is absorbed into the tagged-sum constructors, so `extOrder` is a pure carrier-shape transformation. Paired with `restrictOrder` it implements the LN's strict `extends` relation: `restrictOrder (extOrder lt) = lt` holds definitionally by case four.
